report_name: Russian APTs Re-Exploiting Past Breaches for Renewed Attacks in Ukraine

threat_actors.name: APT28
threat_actors.description: A Russian state-sponsored threat actor systematically revisiting previously compromised networks in Ukraine to establish long-term persistence. The group has evolved its social engineering tactics to include direct voice and video calls to build rapport before malware delivery.

threat_actors.name: Void Blizzard
threat_actors.description: A Russian state-sponsored hacking group reported by CERT-UA to be involved in systematically revisiting previously compromised networks in Ukraine to launch follow-up operations.

attack_patterns.name: Obtain Capabilities
attack_patterns.description: Threat actors are reusing previously compromised infrastructure, credentials, and access from past breaches to launch new attacks, saving the resources needed to establish a new foothold.

attack_patterns.name: Spearphishing Voice
attack_patterns.description: APT28 engages targets via phone or video calls to build trust before sending a malicious file or link through a messaging app, increasing the likelihood of execution.

attack_patterns.name: Create Account
attack_patterns.description: Mentioned as a potential persistence technique used by attackers to maintain long-term access to compromised networks after re-establishing a foothold.

attack_patterns.name: Web Shell
attack_patterns.description: Attackers may leave behind web shells as a form of persistence, which they check for during their re-exploitation campaigns.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts
attack_patterns.description: Attackers methodically check if stolen credentials (usernames, passwords, tokens) from previous breaches are still valid to regain access to networks.

identities.name: Ukrainian Government Institutions
identities.description: Targeted by Russian APTs who revisit old breaches to launch new attacks and maintain persistence for espionage and disruptive operations.

identities.name: Ukrainian Armed Forces
identities.description: Targeted by Russian APTs who revisit old breaches to launch new attacks and maintain persistence for espionage and disruptive operations.

identities.name: CERT-UA
identities.description: Ukraine's Computer Emergency Response Team, which issued the warning about the tactical shift by Russian state-sponsored threat actors.

infrastructure.name: Persistent Access Backdoors
infrastructure.description: Backdoors from previous compromises that are checked by attackers to see if they are still active for reuse in new campaigns.

indicators.name: Re-activation of Previously Disabled Account
indicators.pattern:
title: Re-activation of Previously Disabled Account
id: 2b1a9c8d-f3e4-4d5c-8a7b-9e0f1d2c3b4a
status: experimental
description: Detects the re-enabling of a user account that was previously disabled. This could indicate an attacker re-using old access by re-activating a dormant account, as described in CERT-UA warnings about Russian APT tactics.
references:
  - https://therecord.media/ukraine-warns-russian-hackers-are-revisiting-past-breaches
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-04
tags:
  - attack.t1078
  - attack.credential_access
level: medium
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 4722
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate re-activation of a former employee's or contractor's account.
indicators.description: Detects the re-enabling of a user account that was previously disabled. This behavior was highlighted by CERT-UA as a potential indicator of attackers re-using access from old breaches.
